author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew XII. 9, 10.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And when He was departed thence, He went into their synagogue: and, behold, a man which had his hand withered.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again He heals on a Sabbath day, vindicating what had been done by His disciples.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the other evangelists indeed say, that He “set” the man “in the midst,” and asked them, “If it was lawful to do good on the Sabbath days.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See the tender bowels of the Lord.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “He set him in the midst,” that by the sight He might subdue them; that overcome by the spectacle they might cast away their wickedness, and out of a kind of shame towards the man, cease from their savage ways.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But they, ungentle and inhuman, choose rather to hurt the fame of Christ, than to see this person made whole: in both ways betraying their wickedness; by their warring against Christ, and by their doing so with such contentiousness, as even to treat with despite His mercies to other men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And while the other evangelists say, He asked the question, this one saith, it was asked of Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And they asked Him,” so it stands, “saying, Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath days? that they might accuse Him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And it is likely that both took place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For being unholy wretches, and well assured that He would doubtless proceed to the healing, they hastened to take Him beforehand with their question, thinking in this way to hinder Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this is why they asked, “Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath days?” not for information, but that “they might accuse Him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet surely the work was enough, if it were really their wish to accuse Him; but they desired to find a handle in His words too, preparing for themselves beforehand an abundance of arguments.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But He in His love towards man doth this also: He answers them, teaching His own meekness, and turning it all back upon them; and points out their inhumanity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He “setteth” the man “in the midst;” not in fear of them, but endeavoring to profit them, and move them to pity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when not even so did He prevail with them, then was He grieved, it is said, and wroth with them for the hardness of their heart, and He saith,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “What man is there among you that shall have one sheep, and if this fall into a pit on the Sabbath days, will he not lay hold of it, and lift it out?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How much then is a man better than a sheep?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore it is lawful to do wellon the Sabbath days.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, lest they have ground of obstinacy, and of accusing him again of transgression, He convicts them by this example.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And do thou mark, I pray thee, how variously and suitably in each case, He introduces His pleas for the breaking of the sabbath.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, first, in the case of the blind man, He doth not so much as defend Himself to them, when He made the clay: and yet then also were they blaming Him; but the manner of the creation was enough to indicate the Lord and Ownerof the law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Next, in the case of the paralytic, when he carried his bed, and they were finding fault, He defends Himself, now as God, and now as man; as man, when He saith, “If a man on the Sabbath day receive circumcision, that the law should not be broken;” (and He said not “that a man should be profited”); “are ye angry at me, because I have made a man every whit whole on the Sabbath day?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As God again, when He saith, “My Father worketh hitherto, and I work.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when blamed for His disciples, He said, “Have ye not read what David did, when he was an hungered, himself and they that were with him, how he entered into the house of God, and did eat the show-bread?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He brings forward the priests also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And here again; “Is it lawful to do good on the Sabbath days, or to do evil?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which of you shall have one sheep?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He knew their love of wealth, that they were all taken up with it, rather than with love of mankind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And indeed the other evangelist saith,that He also looked about upon them when asking these questions, that by His very eye He might win them over; but not even so did they become better.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet here He speaks only; whereas elsewhere in many cases He heals by laying on of hands also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But nevertheless none of these things made them meek; rather, while the man was healed, they by his health became worse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For His desire indeed was to cure them before him, and He tried innumerable ways of healing, both by what He did in their presence, and by what He said: but since their malady after all was incurable, He proceeded to the work.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Then saith He to the man, Stretch forth thine hand.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And he stretched it forth, and it was restored whole, like as the other.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then did they?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They go forth, it is said, and take counsel together to slay Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “the Pharisees,” saith the Scripture, “went out and held a council against Him, how they might destroy Him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They had received no injury, yet they went about to slay Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So great an evil is envy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not against strangers only, but even against our own, is it ever warring.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And Mark saith, they took this counsel with the Herodians.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then doth the gentle and meek One?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He withdrew, on being aware of it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But when Jesus knew their devices, He withdrew Himself,” it is said, “from them.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Where now are they who say, miracles ought to be done?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, by these things He signified, that the uncandid soul is not even thereby persuaded; and He made it plain that His disciples too were blamed by them without cause.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This however we should observe, that they grow fierce especially at the benefits done to their neighbors; and when they see any one delivered either from disease or from wickedness, then is the time for them to find fault, and become wild beasts.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus did they calumniate Him, both when He was about to save the harlot, and when He was eating with publicans, and now again, when they saw the hand restored.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But do thou observe, I pray thee, how He neither desists from His tender care over the infirm, and yet allays their envy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And great multitudesfollowed Him, and He healed them all; and He charged them that were healed, that they should make Him known to no man.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because, while the multitudes everywhere both admire and follow Him, they desist not from their wickedness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, lest thou shouldest be confounded at what is going on, and at their strange frenzy, He introduces the prophet also, foretelling all this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so great was the accuracy of the prophets, that they omit not even these things, but foretell His very journeyings, and changes of place, and the intent with which He acted therein; that thou mightest learn, how they spake all by the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if the secrets of men cannot by any art be known, much more were it impossible to learn Christ’s purpose, except the Spirit revealed it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith the prophet?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, it is subjoined: “That it might be fulfilled which was spoken by Esaias the Prophet, saying, Behold my servant, whom I have chosen; my beloved, in whom my soul is well pleased.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I will put my Spirit upon Him, and He shall show judgment to the Gentiles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He shall not strive nor cry,neither shall any man hear His voice in the streets.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: A bruised reed shall He not break, and smoking flax shall He not quench, till He send forth judgment unto victory.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in His name shall the Gentiles trust.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The prophet celebrates His meekness, and His unspeakable power, and opens to the Gentiles “a great door and effectual;” he foretells also the ills that are to overtake the Jews, and signifies His unanimity with the Father.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “behold,” saith He, “my servant, whom I have chosen, my beloved, in whom my soul is well pleased.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now if He chose Him, not as an adversary doth Christ set aside the law, nor as being an enemy of the lawgiver, but as having the same mind with Him, and the same objects.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then proclaiming His meekness, he saith, “He shall not strive nor cry.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For His desire indeed was to heal in their presence; but since they thrust Him away, not even against this did He contend.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And intimating both His might, and their weakness, he saith, “A bruised reed shall He not break.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed it was easy to break them all to pieces like a reed, and not a reed merely, but one already bruised.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And smoking flax shall He not quench.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Here he sets forth both their anger that is kindled, and His might that is able to put down their anger, and to quench it with all ease; whereby His great mildness is signified.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Shall these things always be?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And will He endure them perpetually, forming such frantic plots against Him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Far from it; but when He hath performed His part, then shall He execute the other purposes also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this He declared by saying “Till He send forth judgment unto victory: and in His name shall the Gentiles trust.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As Paul likewise saith, “Having in a readiness to revenge all disobedience, when your obedience is fulfilled.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what is, “when He sends forth judgment unto victory?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When He hath fulfilled all His own part, then, we are told, He will bring down upon them His vengeance also, and that a perfect vengeance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then shall they suffer His terrors, when His trophy is gloriously set up, and the ordinances that proceed from Him have prevailed, and He hath left them no plea of contradiction, however shameless.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He is wont to call righteousness, “judgment.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not to this will His dispensation be confined, to the punishment of unbelievers only, but He will also win to Himself the whole world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore He added, “And in His name shall the Gentiles trust.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, to inform thee that this too is according to the purpose of the Father, in the beginning the prophet had assured us of this likewise, together with what had gone before; saying, “My well-beloved, in whom my soul is well pleased.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For of the well-beloved it is quite evident that He did these things also according to the mind of the beloved.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Then they brought unto Him one possessed with a devil, blind and dumb, and He healed him, insomuch that the blind and dumb both spake and saw.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: O wickedness of the evil spirit! he had barred up both entrances, whereby that person should have believed, as well sight as hearing; nevertheless, both did Christ open.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And all the people were amazed, saying, Is not this the Son of David?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But the Pharisees said, This fellow doth not cast out devils, but by Beelzebub, the prince of the devils.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet what great thing had been said?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless, not even this did they endure: to such a degree, as I have already remarked, are they ever stung by the good works done to their neighbors, and nothing grieves them so much as the salvation of men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet He had actually retired, and had given room for their passion to subside; but the evil was again rekindled, because a benefit was again conferred; and the evil spirit was not so indignant as they.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he indeed departed from the body, and gave place and fled away, uttering no sound; but these were endeavoring now to slay, now to defame Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, their first aim not succeeding, they would fain hurt His good name.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Such a thing is envy, than which no worse evil can exist.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the adulterer indeed enjoys some pleasure, such as it is, and in a short time accomplishes his proper sin; but the envious man punishes himself, and takes vengeance upon himself more than on the person whom he envies, and never ceases from his sin, but is continually engaged in the commission thereof.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as a sow in mire, and evil spirits in our hurt, so also doth he delight in his neighbor’s ills; and if anything painful take place, then is he refreshed, and takes breath; accounting the calamities of others his own joys, and the blessings of others his own ills; and he considers not what pleasure may accrue to himself, but what pain to his neighbor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These men therefore were it not meet to stone and beat to death, like mad dogs, like destroying demons, like the very furies?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as beetles feed on dung, so do these men on the calamities of others, being a sort of common foes and enemies of our nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whereas the rest of mankind pity even a brute when it is killed, dost thou, on seeing a man receive benefits, become like a wild beast, tremble, and turn pale?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, what can be worse than this madness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore, you see, whoremongers and publicans were able to enter into the kingdom, but the envious, being within it, went out: For “the children of the kingdom,” it is said, “shall be cast out.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the former, once freed from their present wickedness, attained to things which they never looked for, while these latter lost even the good things which they had; and very reasonably.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this turns a man into a devil, this renders one a savage demon.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus did the first murder arise; thus was nature forgotten; thus the earth defiled; thus afterwards did it open its mouth, to receive yet living, and utterly destroy, Dathan, and Korah, and Abiram, and all that multitude.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But to declaim against envy, one may say, is easy; but we ought to consider also how men are to be freed from the disease.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then are we to be rid of this wickedness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If we bear in mind, that as he who hath committed fornication cannot lawfully enter the church, so neither he that envies; nay, and much less the latter than the former.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as things are, it is accounted even an indifferent thing; wherefore also it is little thought of; but if its real badness be made evident, we should easily refrain from it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Weep then, and groan; lament, and entreat God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Learn to feel and to repent for it, as for a grievous sin.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou be of this mind, thou wilt quickly be rid of the disease.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And who knows not, one may say, that envy is an evil thing?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: No one indeed is ignorant of it: yet they have not the same estimation of this passion as of adultery and fornication.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When, at least, did any one condemn himself bitterly for having envied? when did he entreat God concerning this pest, that He would be merciful to him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: No man at any time: but if he shall fast and give a little money to a poor man, though he be envious to the thousandth degree, he counts himself to have done nothing horrid, held as he is in subjection by the most accursed passion of all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence, for example, did Cain become such as he was?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence Esau?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence the children of Laban?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence the sons of Jacob?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence Korah, Dathan, and Abiram, with their company?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence Miriam?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence Aaron?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence the devil himself?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Herewith consider this also; that thou injurest not him whom thou enviest, but into thyself thou art thrusting the sword.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For wherein did Cain injure Abel?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Did he not even against his own will send him the more quickly into the kingdom? but himself he pierced through with innumerable evils.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherein did Esau harm Jacob?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Did not Jacob grow wealthy, and enjoy unnumbered blessings; while he himself both became an outcast from his father’s house, and wandered in a strange land, after that plot of his?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And wherein did Jacob’s sons again make Joseph the worse, and this, though they proceeded even unto blood? had not they to endure famine, and encounter peril to the utmost, whereas he became king of all Egypt?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the more thou enviest, the more dost thou become a procurer of greater blessing to the object of thine envy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For there is a God who beholds these things; and when He sees him injured, that doeth no injury, him He exalts the more, and so makes him glorious, but thee He punishes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if them that exult over their enemies, He suffer not to go unpunished (“For rejoice not,” it is said, “when thine enemies fall, lest at any time the Lord see it, and it displease Him”); much more such as envy those who have done no wrong.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us then extirpate the many-headed wild beast.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in truth many are the kinds of envy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, if he that loves one that is a friend to him hath no more than the publican, where shall he stand who hates him that doeth him no wrong? and how shall he escape hell, becoming worse than the heathens?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also I do exceedingly grieve, that we who are commanded to copy the angels, or rather the Lord of the angels, emulate the devil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed there is much envy, even in the church; and more among us, than among those under authority.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore we must even discourse unto ourselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Tell me then, why dost thou envy thy neighbor?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because thou seest him reaping honor, and words of good report?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then dost thou not bear in mind how much evil honors bring on the unguarded? lifting them up to pride, to vainglory, to arrogance, to contemptuousness; making them more careless? and besides these evils, they wither also lightly away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the most grievous thing is this, that the evils arising therefrom abide immortal, but the pleasure at the moment of its appearing, is flown away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For these things then dost thou envy? tell me.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But he hath great influence with the Ruler, and leads and drives all things which way he will, and inflicts pain on them that offend him, and benefits his flatterers, and hath much power.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These are the sayings of secular persons, and of men that are riveted to the earth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the spiritual man nothing shall be able to hurt.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what serious harm shall he do to him? vote him out of his office?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what of that?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if it be justly done, he is even profited; for nothing so provokes God, as for one to hold the priest’s office unworthily.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if unjustly, the blame again falls on the other, not on him; for he who hath suffered anything unjustly, and borne it nobly, obtains in this way the greater confidence towards God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not then aim at this, how we may be in places of power, and honor, and authority, but that we may live in virtue and self denial.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed places of authority persuade men to do many things which are not approved of God; and great vigor of soul is needed, in order to use authority aright.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as he that is deprived thereof, practises self restraint, whether with or against his will, so he that enjoys it is in some such condition, as if any one living with a graceful and beautiful damsel were to receive rules never to look upon her unchastely.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For authority is that kind of thing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore many, even against their will, hath it induced to show insolence; it awakens wrath, and removes the bridle from the tongue, and tears off the door of the lips; fanning the soul as with a wind, and sinking the bark in the lowest depth of evils.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Him then who is in so great danger dost thou admire, and sayest thou he is to be envied?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, how great madness is here!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Consider, at any rate (besides what we have mentioned), how many enemies and accusers, and how many flatterers this person hath besieging him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Are these then, I pray thee, reasons for calling a man happy?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, who can say so?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But the people,” you say, “hold high account of him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what is this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the people surely is not God, to whom he is to render account: so that in naming the people, thou art speaking of nothing else than of other breakers, and rocks, and shoals, and sunken ridges.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to be in favor with the people, the more it makes a man illustrious, the greater the dangers, the cares, the despondencies it brings with it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For such an one has no power at all to take breath or stand still, having so severe a master.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And why say I, “stand still and take breath”?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Though such an one have never so many good works, hardly doth he enter into the kingdom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For nothing is so wont to overthrowmen, as the honor which comes of the multitude, making them cowardly, ignoble, flatterers, hypocrites.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, for instance, did the Pharisees say that Christ was possessed?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Was it not because they were greedy of the honor of the multitude?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whence did the multitude pass the right judgment on Him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Was it not because this disease had no hold on them?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For nothing, nothing so much tends to make men lawless and foolish, as gaping after the honor of the multitude.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nothing makes them glorious and immoveable, like despising the same.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also great vigor of soul is needed for him who is to hold out against such an impulse, and so violent a blast.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as when things are prosperous, he prefers himself to all, so when he undergoes the contrary, he would fain bury himself alive: and this is to him both hell, and the kingdom, when he hath come to be overwhelmed by this passion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Is all this then, I pray thee, matter of envyings, and not rather of lamentations and tears?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Every one surely can see.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But thou doest the same, in envying one in that kind of credit, as if a person, seeing another bound and scourged and torn by innumerable wild beasts, were to envy him his wounds and stripes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in fact, as many men as the multitude comprises, so many bonds also, so many tyrants hath he: and, what is yet more grievous, each of these hath a different mind: and they all judge whatever comes into their heads concerning him that is a slave to them, without examining into anything; but whatever is the decision of this or that person, this they also confirm.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What manner of waves then, what tempest so grievous as this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, such a one is both puffed up in a moment by the pleasure, and is under water again easily, being ever in fluctuation, in tranquillity never.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, before the time of the assembly, and of the contests in speaking, he is possessed with anxiety and fear; but after the assembly he is either dead with despondency, or rejoices on the contrary without measure; a worse thing than sorrow.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that pleasure is not a less evil than sorrow is plain from the effect it has on the soul; how light it makes it, and unsteady, and fluttering.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this one may see even from those of former times.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When, for instance, was David to be admired; when he rejoiced, or when he was in anguish?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When, the people of the Jews? groaning and calling upon God, or exulting in the wilderness, and worshipping the calf?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore Solomon too, who best of all men knew what pleasure is, saith, “It is better to go to the house of mourning, than to the house of laughter.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore Christ also blesses the one, saying, “Blessed are they that mourn,”but the other sort He bewails, saying, “Woe unto you that laugh, for ye shall weep.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And very fitly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in delight the soul is more relaxed and effeminate, but in mourning it is braced up, and grows sober, and is delivered from the whole swarm of passions, and becomes higher and stronger.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Knowing then all these things, let us shun the glory that comes from the multitude, and the pleasure that springs therefrom, that we may win the real and everlasting glory; unto which may we all attain, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and might, forever and ever.
